front: Poem: 'I know why the caged bird sings!'; the bird's prayer 'from his heart's deep core' is 'a plea, that upward to Heaven he flings'; blood 'red on the cruel bars'
Poem: the title object 'grins and lies' and 'hides our cheeks and shades our eyes'; speakers 'sing, but oh the clay is vile' and 'let the world dream otherwise'
back: We Wear the Mask (Paul Laurence Dunbar)

front: Work: collection dividing poems into standard English 'majors' and dialect 'minors'; William Dean Howells preferred the dialect poems
Poem: speaker tells Miss Lucy to 'quit dat noise' because Malindy's singing 'makes its way in glory / To de very gates of God!'
Poem: 'Thy name is writ on Glory's scroll / In characters of fire'; 'Be proud, my race'; lines used in William Grant Still's Afro-American Symphony
Poem: a tree narrates being haunted by 'the curse of a guiltless man' who was lynched on its branches
Poem: speaker looks into 'the pregnant night' as 'the ship I seek is passing, passing' and his voice 'falls dead a foot from his own lips'
back: Ships That Pass in the Night (Paul Laurence Dunbar)
work: Ships That Pass in the Night
